title: Playwright
summary: Auto-waiting, locators, the page/context/browser model, fixtures, network interception, parallelism, and how Playwright differs from Selenium — with rapid-fire Q&A.

sections.title: What Playwright Is
sections.bodyHtml:
Playwright is a modern end-to-end testing framework from Microsoft. It drives Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit through a single API, talks to browsers over the DevTools/CDP-style protocol rather than the WebDriver wire protocol, and ships official bindings for C#, JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, and Java.

The headline advantage over Selenium: a single process talking to the browser over a persistent connection, with auto-waiting built into every action.

  • Cross-browser via one API (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit).
  • Auto-waiting and web-first assertions reduce flakiness.
  • Built-in network interception, tracing, video, and screenshots.
  • Fast parallel execution out of the box.

sections.title: Browser / Context / Page
sections.bodyHtml:
Browser

A single browser instance. Expensive to launch — you reuse it across tests.

BrowserContext

An isolated session inside the browser — its own cookies, storage, and cache. Like a fresh incognito profile. Cheap to create, so each test gets its own context for isolation. This is how Playwright achieves clean state without relaunching the browser.

Page

A single tab within a context. Most interactions happen here.

Why it matters: reusing one Browser but giving each test its own Context is what makes Playwright both fast and fully isolated — a common interview point.

sections.title: Locators & Auto-Waiting
sections.bodyHtml:
A Locator is a lazy, re-queried handle to an element — it re-resolves the DOM every time you act on it, which avoids stale-element problems.

Recommended, user-facing locators (in priority order):

  • GetByRole — by ARIA role + accessible name; most resilient.
  • GetByLabel, GetByPlaceholder, GetByText.
  • GetByTestId — stable data-testid hooks.
  • CSS / XPath as a fallback.

Auto-waiting: before acting, Playwright waits for the element to be attached, visible, stable, enabled, and able to receive events — so explicit waits and sleep() are rarely needed.

Web-first assertions (Expect(locator).ToBeVisibleAsync()) auto-retry until the condition holds or times out — no manual polling.

await page.GetByRole(AriaRole.Button, new() { Name = "Sign in" }).ClickAsync();
await Expect(page.GetByText("Welcome")).ToBeVisibleAsync();

sections.title: Network Interception & Mocking
sections.bodyHtml:
Playwright can observe and modify network traffic, which is powerful for isolating the UI from flaky backends.

  • page.RouteAsync — intercept a URL pattern and fulfil, modify, or abort the request.
  • Stub an API to return fixed JSON so a UI test is deterministic.
  • Wait for a specific response with page.WaitForResponseAsync.
await page.RouteAsync("**/api/users", async route =>
    await route.FulfillAsync(new() { Status = 200, Body = "[]" }));

sections.title: Fixtures, Config & Auth Reuse
sections.bodyHtml:
• Storage state — log in once, save cookies/localStorage to a file, and load it into new contexts so tests skip the login UI. Big speed win.
  • Config — base URL, browser projects, timeouts, retries, trace/video settings centralized (in C# via fixtures/runsettings, or the JS playwright.config).
  • Projects — run the same suite across multiple browsers / viewports.
Auth pattern: authenticate once in setup, persist storageState, and reuse it — the equivalent of seeding a logged-in session instead of clicking through login per test.

sections.title: Parallelism & CI
sections.bodyHtml:
Playwright runs tests in parallel by default using multiple worker processes; each worker gets its own browser/context, so isolation is preserved. Context-level isolation is exactly what makes safe parallelism possible.

  • Workers scale to CPU cores; tune for the CI runner.
  • Retries for flaky tests, configured centrally.
  • Trace Viewer — record a trace on failure/first-retry; it captures DOM snapshots, network, and console for post-mortem debugging without re-running.
  • Sharding splits the suite across CI machines.

sections.title: Debugging & Tooling
sections.bodyHtml:
• Codegen — records your clicks into a test script with good locators.
  • Trace Viewer — the main triage tool; step through a failed run frame by frame.
  • UI Mode / headed + slowmo for live observation.
  • Auto screenshots and video on failure.

quiz.question: How does Playwright avoid flakiness?
choices: Auto-waiting on every action plus web-first assertions that auto-retry until the condition holds
Inserting fixed sleep() calls between every action to let the DOM settle
Running each test three times and taking the majority result
Polling the network layer until all XHR requests have completed
quiz.correctAnswer: Auto-waiting on every action plus web-first assertions that auto-retry until the condition holds
quiz.explanation: Auto-waiting on every action (attached, visible, stable, enabled) plus web-first assertions that auto-retry until the condition holds or times out.

quiz.question: Browser vs Context vs Page?
choices: Browser, Context, and Page are interchangeable names for a single tab session
Browser = a tab; Context = the instance; Page = isolated cookie storage
Browser = reused instance; Context = isolated session with own cookies/storage; Page = a tab
Browser = isolated session per test; Context = reused instance; Page = the cookie jar
quiz.correctAnswer: Browser = reused instance; Context = isolated session with own cookies/storage; Page = a tab
quiz.explanation: Browser = the instance (reused); Context = isolated session with its own cookies/storage (one per test); Page = a tab.

quiz.question: Why prefer GetByRole over CSS?
choices: It runs faster because role lookups skip the DOM query engine entirely
It matches how users and assistive tech perceive the page, so it resists markup changes
It guarantees a unique match by hashing the element's full XPath
It is the only locator that supports Playwright's auto-waiting mechanism
quiz.correctAnswer: It matches how users and assistive tech perceive the page, so it resists markup changes
quiz.explanation: It matches how users and assistive tech perceive the page, so it's resilient to markup changes and encourages accessible UIs.

quiz.question: How do you reuse login across tests?
choices: Hard-code the session token into playwright.config so every worker shares it
Re-run the login UI steps inside a beforeEach hook for each test
Authenticate once, save storageState (cookies + localStorage), load it into new contexts
Keep a single Context alive and share it across all tests to preserve the session
quiz.correctAnswer: Authenticate once, save storageState (cookies + localStorage), load it into new contexts
quiz.explanation: Authenticate once, save storageState (cookies + localStorage), load it into new contexts so tests skip the login flow.

quiz.question: How does Playwright handle parallelism?
choices: A single worker runs tests sequentially but in separate browser tabs
Multiple worker processes, each with isolated browser context, shardable across CI machines
All tests share one browser context and rely on locking to avoid collisions
It spawns one thread per test inside a single process for lightweight parallelism
quiz.correctAnswer: Multiple worker processes, each with isolated browser context, shardable across CI machines
quiz.explanation: Multiple worker processes, each with isolated browser context; configured by worker count and shardable across CI machines.

quiz.question: Playwright vs Selenium, briefly?
choices: Both use the WebDriver wire protocol, but Playwright adds a nicer assertion library
Selenium has built-in auto-wait and tracing, while Playwright requires manual waits
Single API over a persistent CDP-style connection with built-in auto-wait, mocking, and tracing
Playwright only drives Chromium, whereas Selenium is the only true cross-browser option
quiz.correctAnswer: Single API over a persistent CDP-style connection with built-in auto-wait, mocking, and tracing
quiz.explanation: Single API over a persistent CDP-style connection with built-in auto-wait, network mocking, and tracing — vs Selenium's WebDriver protocol where you assemble waits and tooling yourself.

quiz.question: How do you mock an API in a UI test?
choices: Set a global proxy in the config to redirect all backend calls to a stub server
page.RouteAsync to intercept the request and fulfil it with controlled data
Record the real API response once with the HAR recorder and let Playwright auto-mock it on every later run by default
Use page.WaitForResponseAsync to replace the real response payload with fixed JSON
quiz.correctAnswer: page.RouteAsync to intercept the request and fulfil it with controlled data
quiz.explanation: page.RouteAsync to intercept the request and fulfil it with controlled data, making the test deterministic.

quiz.question: What's the Trace Viewer?
choices: A live dashboard that streams CPU and memory metrics during a test run
A linter that scans your test scripts for fragile locators before execution
A post-mortem tool recording DOM snapshots, network, and console per step to debug failures
A code generator that records your clicks and emits a test script with good locators
quiz.correctAnswer: A post-mortem tool recording DOM snapshots, network, and console per step to debug failures
quiz.explanation: A post-mortem tool that records DOM snapshots, network, and console per step so you debug a failed run without reproducing it.
